entry.title: How to create or change Business Partners using MDG BP Mass Consolidation via custom ABAP program
entry.content:
I am working on SAP MDG – Business Partner Consolidation and trying to create and change Business Partners using the Mass Consolidation framework via a custom ABAP program.

Currently, I am using the class CL_MDC_BP_2_CONSOLIDATION to trigger the Business Partner creation process, and this works as expected for initial creation.

However, when I try to change existing Business Partners using the same consolidation approach, the update is not working. The consolidation process runs, but the BP changes are either not reflected in staging/active area or the update is ignored without errors.

My questions are:

  1. Is Business Partner change supported via MDG BP Consolidation using CL_MDC_BP_2_CONSOLIDATION?
  2. Are there different APIs, classes, or processing steps required for BP change vs BP create in Mass Consolidation?
  3. What is the correct approach / sequence to implement BP create and BP change through a custom ABAP program using MDG Consolidation?
  4. Are there any mandatory mappings, key handling, or flags that must be set specifically for change scenarios?
  5. Is there any recommended SAP standard framework or example for implementing BP mass consolidation (create + change) programmatically?

Any guidance, documentation references, or sample implementation pointers would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: SAPGUI for Windows frontend printing does not send da...
entry.content:
What access method are you using for front-end printing? 
Access Method G is the only one recommended for GUI. 

Please be aware that SAP frontend printing is not designed for mass printing or large documents or any critical printing.  Please see the following note ##128105 and test if the print works correctly with access methods S, C (Windows) U or L (Unix).

128105 - Front-end printing (composite SAP Note) 

   c) Restrictions
      Since frontend printing uses the local GUI connection, it is
      generally unsuitable for the output of large or particularly
      time-critical documents.

entry.title: Re: Uniqueness constraint violation - SAP CPI transport
entry.content: You are changing the package name before promoting to production but the artifacts already exist in production under a different package? If so, you'd have to review your overall workflow because artifacts MUST be unique in the tenant of course.

entry.title: Agentic AI at the 2026 SAP EHS and Product Compliance Conference
entry.content:
On March 24 and 25, TAC Insights and SAP hosted the SAP EHS and Product Compliance Conference in Amsterdam. In a great atmosphere, the SAP EHS and Product Compliance community met in person and benefitted from a full agenda and various networking opportunities. For more information, see this blog post.

One hot topic at the conference was agentic AI and its use in EHS and Product Compliance processes. Agentic AI refers to autonomous systems that act as agents. These make decisions and execute multi-step tasks to achieve specific goals without constant human guidance.

In his conference keynote, Gunther Rothermel, Chief Product Officer and Co-General Manager for SAP Sustainability, explained SAP's product strategy for sustainability. He also described how embedded AI can drive efficiency and reduce operational risks across the product lifecycle. SAP is developing AI agents for EHS and Product Compliance. The EHS Workplace Safety Agent transforms safety observations into continuously mitigated risks and up-to-date safety instructions. It uncovers overlooked hazards, enhances risk assessments, and recommends corrective and preventive actions. The agent automatically generates or updates safety instructions for a safer, more compliant workplace. In Product Compliance, the planned GHS Classification and Labeling Agent supports and accelerates the process of classifying and labeling products based on the Globally Harmonized System (GHS). The agent is based on the latest regulations, reduces manual workload, and minimizes errors. Through seamless integration into the product compliance solution, the agent determines and collects the required input data. It then calculates and proposes the GHS classification and label elements for use in subsequent product compliance processes. The AI agent checks for data gaps and validates data against regulations to ensure accuracy. Users can review the results using explanations of how the agent reached its conclusions.

In a panel discussion, Gunther Rothermel and two SAP customers discussed the real-world impact of AI in EHS and Product Compliance processes. All panelists emphasized the importance of clean and reliable data as a foundation for AI. AI was also part of several customer and partner presentations. These presentations included, for example, the role of AI in safety data sheet authoring. In the Discovery Hub room, attendees could get hands-on experience with the SAP solutions for EHS and Product Compliance. In the EHS room, participants could, for example, try out the AI-assisted compliance management and AI-powered safety instruction generation for risk assessments.

Toward the end of the conference, participants discussed how agentic AI could support or change their work in a lively roundtable. Some SAP customers said that they already use agentic AI to propose potential substance substitutions, explain classifications through a Hazard Communication Chatbot, and support recipe development. One customer expressed concerns that the use of AI is restricted in European countries due to labor laws. The customer also noted that they don't want to share their formulations in the cloud, for example, for AI-based classifications.

When asked about ideas and wishes for use cases supported by agentic AI, customers discussed the following:

  • AI-supported analytics could provide implications for the whole product portfolio after a regulatory change. Going one step further, AI could propose potential future regulatory changes and their potential impact on the product portfolio.
  • AI could simulate compliance before a product is released.
  • Some sustainability requirements are vague, complicated, and data is often missing. AI could propose values for missing data. In general, AI could help identify data gaps and clean up data.
  • AI could help analyze customer requests.
  • Safety data sheets contain a lot of information and are provided in various formats. AI could extract the key information.
  • SAP’s new Product Compliance solution uses a different data model than the classic solution. For the migration from the classic to the new solution, data needs to be mapped. AI could support this mapping.

What do you think about these use cases? Do you have additional ideas? Provide them as comments on this blog post.

entry.title: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Overview

An attendance quota represents an employee's authorized entitlement to work specific types of hours, such as overtime, within a set period based on prior approval.

Attendance Quota can be created for various use cases such as overtime, work from home, higher duties, working outside of flexible bandwidth, etc. To provide flexibility in configuring Attendance Quota for various use cases, we provide an admin configuration to define the template presented to the employee for requesting Attendance Quota.

Template for Attendance Quota

Attendance Quota can be configured by administrators by defining a template with following parameters:

  • Quota Limit - Quota limit can be set when admins want employees to provide a threshold limit on the Attendance Quota. For Ex: When an overtime as Attendance Quota is provided for employees to request, it may require an upper threshold that the employee should not exceed.
  • Time Restriction - Admins can set time restrictions similar to that of Time Segments in the Time Valuation where a Start and End Time can be defined and only when the employee works during this time, it is considered for Attendance Quota. For Ex: If the time restriction is defined as 7 PM to 9 PM, then any recorded time by the employees during this time is considered for calculation of Attendance Quota.
  • Working Day Restriction - Admins can set working day restrictions as Working Day or Non- Working Day. This filter is similar to the Current Day Filter in Time Records Filter. For Ex: If a Working Day Restriction is defined as Working Day, any recorded time during working day will be considered for Attendance Quota calculations.
  • Allowed Weekdays Restriction - Admins can set weekday restriction as days of the week similar to the Weekday filter in Time Records Filter. For Ex: If Weekday restriction is defined as say Monday, Tuesday or Wednesday, then when employee records say overtime on Thursday will not be considered for Attendance Quota.

To allow employees to request for Attendance Quota, the administrators can set templates for the above parameters in Attendance Quota Type. When a Quota limit is provided in the template, a time account type should be defined. This will help in checking for the upper threshold from the time account created for the employee as the Attendance Quota period may be different than the time sheet period.
Ex: I want to provide employee the flexibility to request an attendance quota based all the above parameters, then I would define the template as below


In the above screenshot, I have defined all the parameters with Yes except for Reason. If you wish that employee provides a reason for requesting the Attendance Quota, then the reason field can also be enabled.

Creating an Attendance Quota Type is the first step to define the template for Attendance Quota. Let's see different varieties to suit different use cases.
When an admin wants employees to request for a pre-approval for the below cases, the Attendance Quota would look the following way

Working from Home request


In the above screenshot, for Work from Home, it should be recorded on a working day without any quota limit and for a specific time period.

Overtime Request


In the above screenshot, for Overtime, it has many parameters enabled for employees to select during their request for overtime.
Once the Attendance Quota Type is created, various variables are created automatically.

  • Time Valuation Variable Definition
  • Time Valuation Global Variable Value

Configure Time Valuation for Attendance Quota

To understand the concept on how the Attendance Quota is now configured in Time Valuation, it is important to understand the variables.
Based on the Attendance Quota Type, setting various parameters to "Yes", creates different variables used to configure Time Valuation. The following variables are created with prefix

  • DOES_QUOTA_EXIST for Time Period. This is required in Time Valuation to check if the Attendance Quota is valid for the day of recording.
  • QUOTA_LIMIT for upper threshold value. This is required in Time Valuation to set the threshold of an Attendance Quota and the threshold value is valid only for the time period requested.
  • TIME_SEGMENTS for Time Restriction. This is required in Time Valuation to set the start and end time in the Time Segment Filter.
  • WEEKDAYS for Weekday Restriction. This is required in Time Valuation to set the weekdays in Time Records Filter.
  • WORKING_DAY for Working Day or Non-Working Day. This is used in Time Valuation to set the Current Day Filter in Time Records Filter.

For Ex: Considering a Work from Home Attendance Quota Type which has the External Code as WFH, the following variables are created as Time Valuation Global Variable Values.

DOES_QUOTA_EXIST_WFH
WORKING_DAY_WFH


This Time Valuation Global Variable Value has a Variable Value "No". This is by default and is applicable for employees assigned with Attendance Quota when there is no Attendance Quota with valid time period. Let's say an employee requests for an Attendance Quota from March 15th to March 20th 2026, then on March 23rd 2026, the Attendance Quota is not valid and the value of the variable will be "No". Also, before March 15th 2026, the Attendance Quota is not valid. The Attendance Quota is "Yes" on March 15th until March 20th 2026. So, the Time Valuation Global Variable Value has an effective date and ensures that the same Attendance Quota Type can be used for various employees for different periods.

Similarly, there is another global variable created

Since Work From Home had a Working Day Restriction also set to "Yes" in the Attendance Quota Type, the WORKING_DAY_WFH is created. This has the value as "No Selection" meaning that both Working Day and Non-Working Day are valid when there is no Attendance Quota. 

On similar lines, for the OverTime Attendance Quota Type, there are variables created with

QUOTA_LIMIT_OVERTIME
TIME_SEGMENTS_OVERTIME
WEEKDAYS_OVERTIME
WORKING_DAY_OVERTIME

Using the above variables, let's configure the time valuation.

Use Case: Employee can request an overtime with Time Restriction, Working day or Weekday restriction along with upper threshold or quota limit. 

Following are the Time Valuations that are required to be created. The below valuations are for reference and can be configured with a different order.

Step 1: Check whether Attendance Quota is valid.

In the time valuation configuration, Route Input based on Decision provides a Boolean result (true/false). New fields Comparison Type is introduced and can be selected as Type - Variable. With the selection of the Comparison Type as Variable, the Comparison Variable will be visible. Select the variable - DOES_QUOTA_EXIST_OVERTIME. The output of the Time Type Group in the above case is Time Type Group Above should also have the reference of the Attendance Quota Type as shown below.

If the Attendance Quota is valid for a certain recording on a particular date for an employee, it returns true and the Time Type Group Above gets the value of the overtime. If the Attendance Quota is not valid for a certain recording on a particular date for an employee, it returns false and the Time Type Group Below gets the value.

Step 2: Time Restriction for Attendance Quota

There is a Time Segment Variable Filter that can be assigned to support Attendance Quota where time restriction is enabled. Currently, only one such filter is supported for the variable. If you as an Administrator wish to have Time Segment Filter as well, then it will work as a union where both the filters are considered for Time Valuation calculations. Cross midnight scenario is also supported similar to the definition of the Time Segment Filter with the Next Day option.
Time Type Group Below that will carry the output should also have the Attendance Quota Type as below

Step 3: Weekday and Working Day Restriction for Attendance Quota

There are Weekday and Current Day Variable in the Time Records Variable Filter section in Time Records Filter. The Current Day Variable works similar to the Current Day Filter Attributes an the Weekday Variable works similar to the Weekday Filter.

Step 4: Quota limit for Attendance Quota

In the Time Valuation having Threshold Type, a new type is introduced called Variable. Once the Threshold Type is selected as Variable, a Threshold Variable field can be filled with the QUOTA_LIMIT_OVERTIME variable. The Valuation Method should be Valuate Whole Sheet or Period. This will consider the Attendance Quota period to check the threshold of the variable.

The input Time Type Group when checking the Attendance Quota for the quota period should now be converted to a Time Container as shown below

Time Type Group Below and the Time Type Group Above should have the reference of Attendance Quota Type.

Let’s now check how the Time Recording will look.

This is a basic one where Overtime is retrieved from working time recorded in the first step of the time valuation. This step doesn’t have any Attendance Quota required to be defined in the Time Type Group. If now the output of the first step in time valuation requires to be configured for Attendance Quota, then it is important to check if Attendance Quota is valid in second step of time valuation.

If Attendance Quota is valid, then if filters like Time Segment, Weekday and Working Day in Time Records Filter are required, can be configured as covered in third step of time valuation. The filtered result as output from the third step of time valuation is now converted to Time Container input to the fourth step in time valuation. The threshold value defined in the fourth step of time valuation is required to be calculated for the entire time period of Attendance Quota which could span across time sheets.

It is also possible to continue the chain in Attendance Quota by using the output within the Attendance Quota limit for calculating thresholds defined for a time sheet week with a certain value. To do this, the Time Type Group Below is used as the input in the fifth step of time valuation.

In this time valuation, the Attendance Quota deducted is considered as the input time type group. Pay of the employee for Attendance Quota will depend on the Input Time Type Group and the threshold set for the entire Time Sheet week. Valuation Result Category is set to Attendance Quota Deduction and the time account deductions are recorded. This way, the balance from the time account will help to check if the quota exceeded or is within the limit.

Let's now look at how the employee now requests an Attendance Quota and calculations in the time valuation.

Employee creates an Attendance Quota as follows

Once the Attendance Quota is created, approval from the Approver has been granted, the employee specific variables are created as well.

Each of the variables will have specific time period of Attendance Quota with the value of the variable. Example for Quota Limit is shown below. The value of the Quota Limit was 7 and it is vali from 2nd March to 15th March 2026.

Let's now dive into the working of the Attendance Quota when a time recording is performed.

A recording of Working Time from 8 AM to 6 PM and later another Working Time from 7 PM to 8:15 PM. Since, the employee had requested Attendance Quota with Working Day and time restriction to be considered as 7 PM to 9 PM, the recording from 7 PM to 8:15 PM is considered. The Quota limit is now deducted from 7 hours to 5 hr 45 mins. This can be checked in the Attendance Quota page of the employee.

The same can be checked in Time Account whether the deduction is correct as below.

After many more recordings by the employee, there is a requirement to show that the Attendance Quota limit has been exceeded and only the values which were below the entitlement should be considered every time sheet week to be considered for payroll processing. 

The Attendance Recording have been recorded in different time sheet weeks. It respects the Attendance Quota period for the entitlements. 

Future Enhancements:

  • Support overriding the Overtime Compensation Variant in Job Information.
  • Deleting of Attendance Quota where quota limit is defined.
  • Flexibility to post to Time off in lieu using above time type group or to Time Account defined in Attendance Quota using the below time type group configured in the same Time Valuation.

Conclusion:

Attendance Quota is useful when employers want to provide employees the flexibility to request for an approval prior to working on overtime, work from home or perform higher duties. The employer can confidently configure Attendance Quota and know earlier to the recording of additional time by the employee. This gives flexibility for the employees to take a prior approval by an approver before work. This also relieves the pain of approval after recording hours. Hence, the employee now feels confident on submission of time sheets and amount paid. 

Reference

For more details on the Attendance Quota and its related permissions, please refer to the help documentation - Creating an Attendance Quota | SAP Help Portal and Attendance Quota | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Integration Suite Authentication
entry.content:
Hi,

I have completed the Integration Suite Mission with the trial account and then continued setup the real integration of our api within our respective subaccount. Everything is setup and now I am not able to authenticate against the API. Our API has an API-Key mechanism and I want to disable all auth or simplify the auth on the SAP side of things. In the mission there was a booster setting everything up. In reality this is not really documented in an obvious way.

I am now running into the following error testing the api:

<!doctype html><html lang="en"><head><title>HTTP Status 401 – Unauthorized</title><style type="text/css">body {font-family:Tahoma,Arial,sans-serif;} h1, h2, h3, b {color:white;background-color:#525D76;} h1 {font-size:22px;} h2 {font-size:16px;} h3 {font-size:14px;} p {font-size:12px;} a {color:black;} .line {height:1px;background-color:#525D76;border:none;}</style></head><body><h1>HTTP Status 401 – Unauthorized</h1></body></html>

How can I assign the ESBMessaging.send Role? I tried setting up a service instance but cannot get the OAuth flow to work since I cannot create OAuth2 Credentials inside the  SAP Process Integration Runtime looking the one from the mission. 

JSON View of the Service Key to be created is missing token url, clientID, clientSecret....

{
    "key-type": "client_id_secret",
    "X.509": "",
    "certificate-pinning": true,
    "validity": 365,
    "key-length": 2048
}

Ideal solution would be to completely skip AUTH on this side of things since our api is secured.

entry.title: Sustainable and ESG‑Focused Sourcing - A Practical Guide for Buying Organisations
entry.content:
Procurement teams are being asked to do two difficult things at once.

Move faster. Reduce cost.
And prove responsibility across long, complex supply chains.

Both demands arrive together. Neither can be postponed.

This tension now shapes everyday sourcing decisions. It influences which suppliers are chosen, how contracts are written, and how risk is understood. Sustainable and ESG‑focused sourcing is no longer a future ambition. It is a present‑day capability that buying organisations must make work in practice.

The challenge is not intent. Most organisations already agree on the direction.
The challenge is turning that direction into consistent behaviour.

 

When Responsibility Becomes Operational

Sustainability often enters the organisation through strategy.

It appears in executive statements, policies, and long‑term targets.
At that level, it feels distant from daily buying work.

Then it reaches procurement.

A sourcing team reviews a competitive bid. The price is strong, delivery is proven, but the supplier cannot explain labour practices beyond tier one. The award is delayed, not cancelled. The conversation changes.

A category manager realises a low‑cost option carries regulatory exposure in two regions. The savings look smaller once future risk is discussed.

These are not theoretical debates. They are decisions made under time pressure, with incomplete information.

This is where ESG stops being abstract and becomes operational. Procurement is where responsibility meets reality.

What ESG Means in a Buying Context

In procurement, ESG becomes concrete very quickly.

Environmental considerations show up as emissions, energy use, waste, water, and materials.
Social considerations surface through working conditions, safety, human rights, and inclusion.
Governance considerations appear in ethics, transparency, compliance, and accountability.

None of this replaces price, quality, or delivery. Those fundamentals still matter.

What changes is the frame around them.

A supplier with strong governance is more predictable under pressure.
A supplier investing in environmental performance signals long‑term viability.
A supplier that treats people well is often more stable and reliable.

ESG‑focused sourcing expands how value is assessed. It does not dilute commercial judgement. It sharpens it.

Why Buying Organisations Can No Longer Step Back

It is tempting to treat ESG as someone else’s responsibility.

Sustainability teams define targets.
Legal teams interpret regulation.
Finance teams report the numbers.

Procurement sits between these functions and the supply market.

That position brings influence, but also exposure.

Regulation increasingly focuses on supply chains, not just corporate operations.
Customers and investors expect transparency beyond direct suppliers.
Disruptions reveal which partners are resilient and which are not.

In practice, suppliers with weak environmental, social, or governance practices tend to fail first. Poor oversight hides problems. Unsafe working conditions increase disruption. Weak ethics raise the cost of recovery.

Sustainable sourcing, then, is not only about doing the right thing. It is about keeping the organisation operating when conditions change.

From Policy to Behaviour

Most organisations begin in the same way.

They publish sustainability policies.
They issue supplier codes of conduct.
They define high‑level ambitions.

This work matters. It sets direction.

But it rarely changes behaviour on its own.

The shift happens when ESG expectations are embedded into everyday sourcing processes. Supplier selection criteria change. Requests for proposal ask different questions. Contracts reflect responsibilities that were once implied rather than enforced.

At first, this slows things down.

Teams pause more often.
Suppliers question new requirements.
Data is incomplete or inconsistent.

This is usually the moment of doubt.

Some stakeholders ask whether the effort is worth it. Others worry about cost, speed, or complexity. A sourcing cycle stretches longer than planned. A supplier withdraws rather than adapt.

Early ESG initiatives often feel heavier before they feel better.

The Turning Point

What follows is rarely dramatic.

Once expectations are clear and consistent, suppliers adapt.
Once criteria are embedded, decisions speed up again.
Once data becomes visible, risk is easier to manage.

The work does not disappear. It becomes routine.

ESG moves from exception to normal practice.

This turning point is easy to underestimate. Sustainable sourcing does not succeed through one‑off initiatives. It succeeds when it becomes part of how procurement already works.

Where Technology Supports Behaviour Change

As supply chains grow deeper and more global, manual oversight reaches its limits.

Spreadsheets struggle with multi‑tier visibility.
Email cannot track corrective actions reliably.
Static reports lag behind real events.

This is where technology plays a quiet but important role.

Sustainable sourcing depends on making responsible choices repeatable, not heroic. That requires systems that embed ESG expectations into daily work rather than treating them as separate checks.

In practice, this starts in sourcing. ESG criteria need to sit directly inside sourcing events, not alongside them. Buyers need to compare suppliers on sustainability alongside commercial factors, using consistent data and agreed weightings.

It continues in contracting. Sustainability commitments only matter when they are enforceable. Clear clauses on ethics, labour standards, emissions, and audit rights translate policy into obligation.

It shows up in buying behaviour. Guided buying steers users toward approved suppliers and products, reinforcing ESG policies at the point of purchase rather than relying on after‑the‑fact controls.

Supplier risk and due diligence matter as well. Continuous monitoring helps organisations identify emerging environmental, labour, or governance issues early, rather than discovering them through disruption or audit findings.

Measurement closes the loop. Procurement data needs to connect to wider ESG reporting so progress can be tracked, gaps identified, and improvement managed over time.

In many organisations, SAP Ariba, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP Sustainability Control Tower support this flow. Not as standalone solutions, but as part of an integrated landscape that links sourcing decisions, supplier data, risk visibility, and enterprise‑level ESG steering.

Even with integrated platforms, ESG sourcing still depends on judgement, trade‑offs, and imperfect data. Technology does not remove tension from procurement decisions—but it makes those tensions visible, explainable, and easier to manage.

The value is not the system itself.
It is the discipline it enables.

 

ESG, Risk, and Resilience Are Linked

Recent disruptions have revealed a consistent pattern.

Suppliers with weak governance tend to communicate late.
Suppliers with poor labour practices face higher disruption.
Suppliers that ignore environmental risk struggle to adapt.

By contrast, suppliers with stronger ESG foundations are often more resilient. They flag issues earlier. They collaborate more openly. They recover faster.

From a procurement perspective, this matters.

Sustainable sourcing becomes a form of risk management. Not defensive, but preventive. It reduces the number of surprises teams must manage under pressure.

When ESG data is visible and connected to supplier decisions, resilience stops being reactive.

Synthesis: When Responsibility Becomes Part of Value

ESG is often discussed as a separate priority. Cost here. Sustainability there. Risk somewhere else.

In practice, these concerns converge.

A supplier that cannot demonstrate basic governance creates risk.
A supplier that ignores environmental impact increases future cost.
A supplier that treats people poorly undermines continuity.

Some suppliers that look “cheap” are already expensive.
Some suppliers that look “responsible” are already competitive.

Sustainable sourcing works when these connections are acknowledged and acted upon.

Procurement is not being asked to solve ESG alone. But it is being asked to make ESG real. That means turning ambition into criteria, policy into behaviour, and values into decisions that can be explained and defended.

When this happens, ESG stops competing with commercial priorities. It becomes part of how value is defined.

Returning to the Original Tension

At the beginning, procurement faced a contradiction.

Move faster and reduce cost.
Act responsibly and manage risk.

That contradiction does not disappear.

What changes is how it is handled.

Sustainable and ESG‑focused sourcing does not remove trade‑offs. It makes them visible, deliberate, and defensible. It allows procurement to explain not just what was bought, but why.

A buyer explains a delayed award.
A category lead accepts a narrower supplier list.
A trade‑off is made—not celebrated, but understood.

In doing so, procurement moves from reacting to pressure to shaping outcomes. Quietly. Consistently. At scale.

The tension remains.
But it becomes manageable.

And that is often the difference between intention and impact.

entry.title: Business Area (GSBER) not updated after Asset change in Purchase Requisition
entry.content:
We are facing an issue in Purchase Requisition (ME51N / ME52N) related to Account Assignment (Category A – Asset).

When a PR is created:

  • The Business Area (GSBER) is derived based on the initially entered Asset.
  • However, if the Asset (ANLN1) is changed afterwards, the Business Area is not updated accordingly.

Questions

  1. Is this standard SAP behavior that GSBER is not re-derived after Asset change in PR?
  2. Is there any standard configuration or setting to enable re-derivation of Business Area?
  3. Does SAP recommend implementing a BAdI (e.g. ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST) to handle this?
  4. Is there any SAP Note addressing this behavior?

Best regards,

entry.title: The execution checks whether call is made within the implementation of behavior definition
entry.content:
Hi everyone.

I'm developing an app using ABAP RAP.

Before code refactoring I have put all of the logic in the Behavior Implementation (BI) Class. There is a part of a method that contains the following:

MODIFY ENTITIES OF my_entity_def IN LOCAL MODE
           ENTITY MyEntity
           CREATE BY \_Assoc
           FIELDS ( PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity AFieldOfTheAssoc)
           AUTO FILL CID
           WITH VALUE #( ( %tky    = VALUE #( PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity = lv_key )
                           %target = VALUE #( ( PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity = lv_key
                                                AFieldOfTheAssoc = lv_some_value) ) ) ).

 Since this piece of code was still in the BI Class, I had no warnings.

But now that class got too long, and refactoring was required. I have to move this piece of code to another class. And then call it from the BI class. The app still works the same as before, but now I get the following warning on the LOCAL word:

The execution checks whether call is made within the implementation of behavior definition ''my_entity_def".

I would like to know the correct way to handle this warning. Thank you all for your help!

EDIT:

When using autocomplete in place of IN LOCAL MODE, I get:

  • PRIVILEDGED
  • or just leave it empty

I tried both variants, but both of them show me a new error:

The field "PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity" of entity "my_entity_def" cannot be modified.

It's weird how this error happens because I'm not modifying PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity, just using it as the parent of my newly created Assoc Entity.

It's likely because in the Behavior Definition for the Assoc Entity, I have

define behavior for my_assoc_entity_def alias Assoc
...
{
  update;
  delete;

  association _Parent;

  field ( numbering : managed, readonly ) AssocUuid;
  field ( readonly ) PrimaryKeyOfMyEntity; //Basically the PK of parent

But that line has to be there, otherwise there is no connection from the assoc entity to its parents.

entry.title: Re: Error when posting usage decision for Quality Inspect...
entry.content: Re‑check the mapping of your usage decision code to follow‑on actions in decentralized EWM. Even if Note 3213938 is applied, the error usually means the UD code is not linked to a valid stock posting category. Correcting this mapping in EWM customizing should resolve the issue.

entry.title: Course update for online self study: Exploring SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere and its open data ecosystem are the foundation for a business data fabric.

For self study, you can use the online course Exploring SAP Datasphere.

This course has now been refreshed and updated with a new structure, containing explanations using texts, videos and new exercise simulations.

You can access this course on learning.sap.com here:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-sap-datasphere

Happy learning with SAP!

entry.title: Re: deactivation of company code
entry.content:
Dear @jerrysam,

Marking a company code as DO NOT USE does not delete it or its historical data. SAP documented workaround is to keep the company code and mark it accordingly and historical journal data can still be analyzed by company code in standard apps

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/ea2e03565eea410ee10000000a441470.html

SAP does not define one fixed standard number for all cases. In Public Cloud, retention is controlled through ILM policies/rules, where you define the minimum and maximum retention periods and the system determines the destruction date accordingly

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/14a1b8ef5ee84741a499e12589206cbb.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/ebb8d92101cf478282f6e29563da4782.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/3c9d33dde6154f71863f994262a584fd.html

Also relevant data can also be archived and later used for audit purposes. SAP documents ILM audit areas specifically for audit retrieval but archiving is handled by the relevant ILM-enabled objects, not by one generic archive company code function

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/398428a277a44a46a9b47322790d8034.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/3789a4256f1248219cf6c84e49e1e046.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-Guided Implementation Workshop Series
entry.content:
SAP HANA Cloud is a fully managed, in-memory cloud database service as part of the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It delivers high-speed processing and real-time analytics, which is especially valuable for data-intensive applications. The platform supports multiple data models, including relational, graph, spatial, JSON, and document store, allowing developers to work with different types of data in one environment. It also includes built-in data virtualization and integration capabilities, enabling organizations to access and combine data from SAP and non-SAP sources.

SAP HANA Cloud provides a comprehensive environment for building intelligent, data-driven solutions. It supports advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities directly within the database, which reduces data movement and accelerates insight generation. Developers can use familiar tools and languages such as SQL, Java, Node.js, and Python to create applications and services.
The platform also includes data lake functionality for storing and processing large volumes of less-frequently accessed data at a lower cost, while still allowing seamless querying alongside in-memory data. Its multi-tier storage approach helps organizations balance performance and cost by automatically managing where data resides. Overall, SAP HANA Cloud enables organizations to modernize their data landscape, support real-time decision-making, and drive digital transformation initiatives.

 

🚀 Empower Your Data Journey: Introducing Our New SAP HANA Cloud Expert Guided Implementation Series 

In today’s fast-paced digital landscape, speed of transformation is a must. Businesses across the globe are evolving to make faster, smarter, and more connected decisions by building intelligent, data-driven solutions using advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities. To support this evolution, we’re excited to announce our renewed series of Expert-Guided Implementation workshops (EGI) from SAP Enterprise Support designed to help you upskill, modernize, and future-proof your business processes. Whether you're looking to modernize legacy systems, harness the power of cloud data, or unlock real-time insights through analytics, this workshop series offers targeted enablement for every stage of your development. 

*The following EGI sessions will run throughout the year, with quarterly updates to the schedule. 
Each session is limited to 30 individual customer participants.  
**Registration links below direct to SAP Learning content for SAP Enterprise Support, which requires a one-time registration with a valid S-user.

Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud

In this live EGI session we will guide you through the SAP HANA Cloud capabilities as DBaaS, demonstrate the key features available in the latest quarterly release of the tool and show the benefits of using the administration tools for DB Monitoring, security and configuration. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP HANA Cloud
  • Overview of SAP BTP
  • Introduction and usage of Administration tools
    • SAP HANA Cloud Central
    • SAP HANA Cockpit
    • SAP HANA Database Explorer
  • Managing, Monitoring and Alert Notification in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Workload Management:
    • Managing Tables
    • HANA NSE
  • SAP HANA HDI Overview
  • Introduction and usage of Cloud Foundry CLI 
  • Security Administration and User management

Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Modeling

In this live EGI session, we will guide you through the process of modeling in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of calculation view usage and design in SAP HANA Cloud utilizing the SAP Business Application studio. Also, we showcase the core capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud for calculation view graphical modelling and provide help on about SQL Script procedures and implementing functions using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Usage of SAP Business Application Studio and Administration tools
  • Deploy SAP Business Application Studio
  • Introduction and modeling Calculation views
  •  Modeling with SAP Business Application Studio and using Database Explorer
  • Modeling features
  • Generating restricted and calculated columns
  • SQL Script usage
  • Performance Analysis, Debug mode & SQL Analyser
  • Overview of Best Practices

 Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph

In this live EGI session, we empower customers with the knowledge of SAP HANA Cloud multiple model programming using the latest concepts including vector engine, knowledge graph engine and retrieval augmented generation. After the completion of the workshop, customers will have the knowledge of multiple model engines and how to incorporate artificial intelligence and retrieval augmented generation capabilities.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understand SAP HANA Cloud architecture and deployment options
  • Introduction to multiple model programming
  • Data Tiering considerations for multiple model programming
  • Overview of SAP Business Application Studio
  • Spatial data concepts in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understanding spatial features and functions
  • Spatial data modeling and processing
  • Graph processing in SAP HANA Cloud
  • JSON document store in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Automated machine learning (AutoML) in SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Cloud vector database
  • Capabilities and benefits of multiple model processing with vectors
  • SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine
  • Knowledge graph and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
  • Real-world applications for Retrieval Augmented Generation

Register Here* 

 

Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule)

In this live EGI session we will empower you with the knowledge of cloud application programming development in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of CAP CDS development and design in SAP HANA Cloud and understand how to build and deploying cloud application projects from scratch and with reduced costs utilizing pro-code development using SAP Build Code with Joule. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda:

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Introduction to SAP Build Code and Prompt Engineering
  • CAP Architecture and SAP Business Application Studio features
  • Build and deploy a CAP project using SAP Business Application Studio
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud using CAP CDS
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Optimization
  • Building Application using SAP Build Code and Joule
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Management and Administration

Register Here* 

Learning Outcomes from the EGI Series:

  • Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud:
    Master SAP HANA Cloud configuration, performance monitoring and secure user management using standard administration tools.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Modeling:
    Develop Calculation views and SQL Script procedures within SAP Business Application Studio.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph:
    Implement advanced Vector Engine, Knowledge Graph, and Spatial Data capabilities to power Generative AI and Retrieval Augmented Generation scenarios
  • Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule):
    Build and deploy end-to-end CAP applications efficiently using SAP Build Code and Joule


Who Should Attend? 

This workshop series is ideal for developers, modelers, business analysts, system administrators, database administrators and SAP customers looking to transform their business processes. Whether you're just getting started or want to go deeper into SAP's modern toolset, there’s a session tailored for your needs. 

 

GET MORE CONTENT LIKE THIS!

 SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan support and empowerment — have a dedicated SAP Community Group.

As part of your SAP cloud subscription, you get access to:

  • Expert-led resources
  • Tailored recommendations
  • Advisory services
  • …all designed to help you accelerate innovation and success.


👉 Visit our Community Group
Sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID, then hit “Subscribe” to stay updated.

Looking to dive deeper into data-driven innovation?
Within the group, explore the Data & Analytics Value Map to connect with your Foundational Support team. Simply click “Join Group”, and once inside, you can even request a call with a topic expert to get personalized guidance and enablement support.

 

 

 

 

 

Quicklinks

  • SAP Business Data Cloud : Expert-Guided Implementation series
  • Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
  • SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps - Community Group
  • SAP Enterprise Support - Guided Learning

entry.title: User Assistance Is Here To Help You!
entry.content:
Since then, we’ve been at work making the best use of the feedback that you gave us, so we wanted very shortly to do just a couple of things here.

First, offer a hearty and sincere THANK YOU for the feedback during and after the session, as well as whenever you take the time out of your busy day to give it. We are always grateful for your perspective as we work to refine and improve what we’re doing for you.

Second, we wanted to reiterate the open call for feedback: Whether it’s via reply to this post, questions or comments on the SAP Community that you tag one of us in, or, maybe best of all, comments left using the feedback tool on the SAP Help Portal, we’re always here to help.

In fact, we've had numerous conversations or other direct feedback about things that are important to you that we've been working on, and are always happy to hear more.

So if you’ve got something on your mind—about a Help Portal topic, or some in-app help content that isn’t cutting it for you, or a question about any What’s New content, or, really, anything, use that feedback feature, comment here, or reach out to us.

We’ll make sure the feedback gets where it needs to and that we’ll get to work on it!

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: SBPA failed when use "Rest Call Web Service with Dest...
entry.content:
Hi,

I get the same error when I try to run it in a specific shared environment, in my case the QAS. But when I run the same project in another shared environment (DEV) the error not occurs.

 

I was looking for the root of problem and when I run in Debug mode with Core SDK equal 1.77.19, everything fine.

 

When I change the CORE SDK version above 1.77.19, the error occurs:

 

I think the problem lies with the SDK CORE version. But when I reverted to version 1.77.19 of the SDK CORE and did a new deployment in the shared QAS environment, the error persists.

entry.title: In the context of People Intelligence, can it consume detailed Performance Mgmt. data from SF

In the context of People Intelligence, can it consume and model detailed Performance Management data from SuccessFactors, such as Continuous Feedback and form-based entities (e.g. FormObjective, FormObjectiveSection, FormCompetency, FormCompetencySection), in order to provide analytical reporting on employee-level ratings (not only overall ratings, but also detailed evaluations)?

We are currently facing limitations when accessing these objects via SAC OData connection (e.g. KBA 3490835, sourcetype is missing in request headers, error 400), so the question is whether People Intelligence can natively leverage this data without relying on exports from SF (Integration Center, Story reports)?

entry.title: Re: Can't add favorites in /IWFND/V4_ADMIN
entry.content:
Unfortunately, only a code fix can solve it.

Here is a temporary fix which works for me, in the program /IWFND/R_V4_ADMIN_SERVICES, in ABAP 7.58 SP1:

The code fix is on the left side in the below screenshots; the ABAP code is also shared as text below the screenshots.

Code as text:

 

[...]

  METHOD handle_context_menu_sel.

    DATA:ls_node TYPE treev_node.            " <========== CHANGE
*    DATA:ls_node TYPE /iwfnd/s_v4_admin_tree.

    READ TABLE mt_tree_node INTO ls_node
      WITH KEY node_key = node_key.

[...]

FORM build_group_tree.

[...]

    ls_item-item_name =  gc_tree_service_group_column-column_service_group.
    ls_item-class = cl_gui_column_tree=>item_class_text.
    ls_item-info     = <ls_favorite>-value.              " <========== ADD
    APPEND ls_item TO mt_tree_item.
  ENDLOOP.

  " Node '/IWBEP/ALL' if exists
  READ TABLE mt_group ASSIGNING <ls_group>

[...]

FORM add_favorite USING is_node TYPE treev_node.     " <========== CHANGE
*FORM add_favorite USING is_node TYPE /iwfnd/s_v4_admin_tree.

  DATA: ls_favorite TYPE /iwfnd/s_v4_ui_favorite.

  DATA(ls_item) = mt_tree_item[ node_key  = is_node-node_key   " <==== ADD
                                item_name = gc_tree_service_group_column-column_service_group ].
  CASE ls_item-level_no.         " <========== CHANGE
*  CASE is_node-level_no.

    WHEN gcs_node_level-group_key OR gcs_node_level-iwbep_all_text.
      ls_favorite-id    = /iwfnd/cl_v4_ui_favorite=>gcs_favorite_id-group_key.
      IF ls_item-level_no = gcs_node_level-group_key. " <========== CHANGE
        ls_favorite-value = ls_item-info.             " <========== CHANGE
*      IF is_node-level_no = gcs_node_level-group_key.
*        ls_favorite-value = is_node-info.
      ELSE.
        ls_favorite-value = /iwfnd/if_v4_publishing_types=>co_group_iwbep_all.
      ENDIF.

    WHEN OTHERS.
      RETURN.

  ENDCASE.

  /iwfnd/cl_v4_ui_favorite=>add( ls_favorite ).

  PERFORM refresh.

ENDFORM.                    " add_favorite


*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  remove_favorite
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM remove_favorite  USING is_node TYPE treev_node.   " <========== CHANGE
*FORM remove_favorite  USING is_node TYPE /iwfnd/s_v4_admin_tree.

  DATA: ls_favorite TYPE /iwfnd/s_v4_ui_favorite.

  DATA(ls_item) = mt_tree_item[ node_key  = is_node-node_key   " <==== ADD
                                item_name = gc_tree_service_group_column-column_service_group ].
  CASE ls_item-level_no.        " <========== CHANGE
*  CASE is_node-level_no.

    WHEN gcs_node_level-favorite_group.
      ls_favorite-id    = /iwfnd/cl_v4_ui_favorite=>gcs_favorite_id-group_key.
      ls_favorite-value = ls_item-info.     " <========== CHANGE
*      ls_favorite-value = is_node-info.

    WHEN gcs_node_level-favorite_serv.
      ls_favorite-id = /iwfnd/cl_v4_ui_favorite=>gcs_favorite_id-service_key.
      CONCATENATE mv_group_id
                  mv_system_alias
                  mv_repository_id
                  mv_service_id
                  mv_service_version
        INTO ls_favorite-value SEPARATED BY ' - '.

    WHEN OTHERS.
      RETURN.

  ENDCASE.

  /iwfnd/cl_v4_ui_favorite=>remove( ls_favorite ).

  DELETE mt_favorite
    WHERE id    = ls_favorite-id
      AND value = ls_favorite-value.
  IF mt_favorite IS INITIAL.
    CLEAR: mv_group_id, mv_repository_id, mv_service_id, mv_service_version.
  ENDIF.

  PERFORM refresh.

ENDFORM.                    " remove_favorite

[...]

 

For information, the favorites are stored in the table /IWFND/I_V4_MFAV.

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud where approval mails are trigred from S/4HANA during the Sales Order approval
entry.content:
Issuer Details :We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud where approval emails are triggered from S/4HANA during the Sales Order approval process.

Requirement: When an approval email is sent, the email should contain a dynamic link that:Opens a specific Fiori applicationAutomatically passes the Sales Order number from the emailDisplays the application pre-filtered for that specific Sales Order

Problem Statement :

Currently, the approval email is sent with a static link to a Fiori application and a Sales Order number mentioned in the email body.However:The link does not pass the Sales Order number dynamicallyApprovers must manually search or filter the Sales Order in the applicationThis leads to usability issues and increases approval processing timeWe want to understand whether S/4HANA Public Cloud

entry.title: Re: How to set up a background job for transaction MR90 (...
entry.content: Hi Lakshmipathi, Thank you for the information, but that is was not enough. In the meantime, I found a solution. The RSNAST00 program must be used and executed only in the background. Additionally, the output type in NACE must be set to at least 1 (Send with periodically scheduled job) in the Dispatch time field. It may seem obvious, but it was quite tricky to finally resolve. Thank you everyone, and I’m closing the topic.

entry.title: Deactive / sell / scrap assets in company codes closed for a long time
entry.content:
We want to delete all assets belong to companies had been sold/closed for a long time. Posting period are all closed for these companies. We tried tcodes ABAVN ABAON F-92 to remove these assets but they tried to create posting and all require company period open. Asset cannot be deleted once transaction exists either. Is there a way to scrap or delete them without any new posting? We just want to delete them from database so to clear FI_DOCUMNT errors and compress ACDOCA.

I also archived some assets. Even DELETION step is run, assets still in ANLA table. How do we delete these assets without creating any posting?

Thanks, Colin

entry.title: DRC for France einvoicing B2G and B2B
entry.content:
We are currently implementing e‑invoicing for our France B2B customers and have encountered an issue in the eDocument Cockpit. The XML is not visible, and after debugging we identified that table EDOEUDOCTYPE was not updated with the expected entries, including EDOC_TYPE FR_B2BINV and other France B2B document types. FR_EUINV is appearing in EDOEUDOCTYPE. 

All relevant OSS Notes have been applied and the configuration steps have been completed, but the table remains unchanged. Could you clarify how EDOEUDOCTYPE is populated and what triggers the update for France B2B e‑invoicing scenarios?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Kitting workflow to support CTO process
entry.content:
The CTO process used in my warehouse has a manual kitting and packaging operation, where the external manufacturing application generates a kit list. The operator looks up the inventory and sends an email to the storage locations to transfer materials to the packaging location. The parts are packed and returned to the location that initiated the request.  I am trying to find a way to implement a system driven process to manage this. I have three constraints that must be adhered to:
CTO process cannot be impacted. Solution must be additive only
Need to govern material flow referencing the order to maintain clarity of process. 
The packing team uses packing instructions in the current outbound parts shipping. The same operation needs to be followed to take advantage of efficiency and packing integrity.
I have reviewed use of Delivery documents without reference, STO between storage locations, sales orders with reservations, production orders.  All options seem to leave a critical gap. If anyone has attempted this with any success using best practices, could you share your approach?

We are using S/4 HANA with stockroom (LE-WM).

I do not want to deploy a partial solution. Any suggestions would be appreciated.

entry.title: Re: SAP Crystal Reports libcurl.dll Vulnerabilities
entry.content: This is not on Microsoft. This is clearly in your installation folder and will not be updated by Microsoft. Ignoring these is not an option. The vulnerable file is at this path. C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP BusinessObjects\Crystal Reports for .NET Framework 4.0\Common\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win64_x64\libcurl.dll

entry.title: Re: Is MCP Necessary? Lessons from AI-Assisted SAP Datasphere Modeling
entry.content:
Great article! 👍

Thank you for sharing this honest comparison. I completely agree with your conclusion — with a mature and well-featured CLI like @sap/datasphere-cli, building a full MCP server often adds unnecessary complexity and cost.

I took a similar lightweight approach and built a custom GitHub Copilot agent that works directly with the CLI through natural language. It focuses on simplicity, safety (especially for destructive actions), and easy adoption inside VS Code with no extra infrastructure. Your post reinforces my decision to keep it simple for now.

Natural Language Automation for SAP Datasphere wit... - SAP Community

entry.title: IF YOU HAD TO RESTART YOUR SAP CAREER TODAY -- CAPM or UI5?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community👋

I am fresher who has recently started my journey in the SAP ecosystem, and I am currently facing an important career decision. I would really appreciate guidance from experienced
professionals in the community.


#The situation
Recently, I was asked to choose between working on :
1)SAP CAPM
2)SAP UI5/SAP FIORI

As someone at the beginning of my career , I want to make an informed decision that will help me ensure long-term career growth and stay aligned with SAP's future roadmap.

However, I am finding it difficult to decide which path would be better in the long run.

##SAP CAPM

From what I understand, SAP CAPM focus on:
1)Backend development
2)Cloud-based applications
3)OData services
4)Business logic
5)SAP BTP development

##SAP UI5/FIORI

SAP UI5 and FIORI appear to focus on:
1)Frontend development
2)UI/UX for enterprise applications
3)SAP Fiori apps
4)User experience design
5)SAP S?4HANA interfaces

##My Career Goals

As a fresher, I aim to:
1)Become a strong SAP developer
2)Stay relevant for future SAP trends
3)Explore opportunities in SAP BTP and cloud

 

###My questions to the community

I would love to hear from experienced SAP professionals:
1)Which path would you recommend for a fresher -- SAP CAPM or SAP UI5?
2)Which technology has better long term growth?
3)Which one offers more opportunities in the SAP ecosystem?
4)Is it better to start with one and later transition to the other?

entry.title: The Importance of Choosing the Right Data Type in Object Definitions
entry.content:
Recently, I encountered a critical issue while working on an object definition that reinforced a simple yet often overlooked concept — data type selection matters more than we think.

In this case, I was working on a custom MDF object Deduction Request (cust_Deduction_Request) in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central.

  • Field Name: cust_Amount
  • Data Type: String

This object was integrated via Integration Center to post data into One-Time Deduction (Pay Component Non-Recurring).

Everything seemed fine initially.

Here’s where things went wrong:

During testing, the integration worked fine because I entered only numeric values (without commas).

However, once it went live, users started entering amounts in a common format like 1,599 (with commas).

Due to this, the integration job started failing.

Error from Integration Centre:

Property value has invalid value. For input string: "1,599", required type is Edm.Double.
Additional One Time Payment & Deductions (EmpPayCompNonRecurring): Property value has invalid value.


What caused the issue:

  • The field was defined as String, so it accepted any format, including commas
  • Users entered values like 1,599
  • However, the target field expects a numeric value (Edm.Double)
  • Because of this mismatch, the integration job failed

Root cause:

On checking the object definition:

The field cust_Amount was defined as String, and there was no validation to restrict formatted numeric input.

We changed the data type from String to Number and re-tested the integration. After this update, the integration worked as expected.
While String fields can accept values in any format (with or without commas), this flexibility can cause issues during integration. Using a Number data type ensures that only valid numeric values are maintained.
This was a small but important correction aligning the data type with how the data is actually used.

Final Thought:

This was a small configuration decision, but it caused issues after go-live when real user input came into picture. It was a good reminder to think beyond testing scenarios and consider actual user behaviour.

entry.title: LGM Knowledge Snippet: Getting Started with FTL
entry.content:
If you've been working with SAP Logistics Management for LTL or Parcel shipping, you're familiar with the consignment-based workflow: Transportation Requests are grouped into consignments, carriers are determined, and goods are picked and packed before dispatch. But what about scenarios where you need an entire truck dedicated to a single shipment? That's where the Full Truck Load (FTL) shipping process comes in, and it just has been made available, Here are some additional infos for the process and it´s current limitations.

What Makes FTL Different?

In FTL shipping, a complete truck (or truck and trailer) is exclusively subcontracted for the shipper's cargo. Unlike LTL, where multiple shipments share carrier capacity and costs are based on weight or volume, FTL costs are driven by the truck itself - the time it's occupied and the route it travels, regardless of how fully it's loaded. We already support the Direct LTL process flow from the beginning (in there, the truck picks up cargo and delivers it to the customer without routing the pallets through the carriers netwrok/warehouses, but the truck is shared with other customers of the carrier) and the FTL process flow is quite similar as of now.

The FTL Process Flow

Here's how the FTL process works step by step:

  1. Logistical Demand arrives - A delivery notification from S/4HANA (or another source) triggers the creation of a Transportation Request
  2. Processing Variant is determined - Based on your Processing Variant configuration rules, the system identifies this shipment as FTL
  3. Freight Order is created automatically - This is the critical difference. Instead of creating a consignment first, the system immediately creates a Freight Order and assigns the Transportation Request to it
  4. Carrier is assigned - A carrier is assigned to the Freight Order, either automatically (if provided with the logistical demand) or manually through the Manage Freight Orders app
  5. Vehicle Type is maintained - To reflect the type of truck used for the freight order, the all new vehicle type has been introduced. So far, only a description and the weight capacity can be maintained in the Manage Vehicle Types app, but this might change in future.

     

  6. Carrier confirmation — The Freight Order is is communicated to the carrier, at the moment this is only supported with a manual process
  7. Warehouse processing is triggered - Only after the carrier confirms does the system release the Transportation Request for warehouse processing. This ensures the warehouse knows exactly which truck to load before they start
  8. Pickup/Delivery Document is created (technically, this happens in parallel to the warehouse processing trigger) - A PDD is created based on the confirmed Freight Order stop at the shipping point, coordinating the physical loading operation
  9. Warehouse execution completes — Goods are picked, packed, and staged for loading
  10. Pickup Execution: The standard processing of the Pickup/Delivery document including CheckIn, Loading and CheckOut is performed..

FTL and Direct LTL: Closer Than You Might Think

As teased before, if you're already familiar with the Direct LTL shipping process, the FTL flow will feel very familiar. In fact, Direct LTL and FTL follow nearly the same process pattern. Both share these key characteristics:

  • Early carrier determination — The carrier is known before warehouse processing begins. Important difference for now: FTL does not (yet) support tendering
  • Carrier confirmation gates warehouse work - In both processes, the system waits for the carrier to confirm before releasing goods for picking and packing
  • PDD is created from the subcontracting document — The Pickup/Delivery Document is generated automatically once the carrier confirms, rather than being matched from a pickup schedule
  • Early TR-to-PDD linking — Transportation Requests are connected to their PDD before warehouse processing starts (not after, as in Network LTL or Parcel)

The key difference between them is the document that orchestrates the process: Direct LTL uses a Consignment as its subcontracting document, while FTL uses a Freight Order. 

The Freight Order: Central Document for FTL

The Freight Order is the central planning and subcontracting document in the FTL process. Think of it as the equivalent of what a consignment is for LTL - but designed specifically for full truck loads. It contains:

  • Stops - Pickup and delivery locations with planned arrival times (at the moment, we support only 2 stops)
  • Partners -The assigned carrier, ship-to party, and other business partners
  • Assigned Transportation Requests - The goods that will be loaded onto this truck
  • Vehicle information - Vehicle type, utilization details, and capacity
  • Status tracking - Lifecycle status, carrier determination status, and carrier confirmation status

You can manage Freight Orders through the Manage Freight Orders app, which provides a list view of all Freight Orders with their key attributes and statuses, as well as a detailed object page for each Freight Order.

There is a new tile (with a slightly different truck icon, special thanks to our designers :-)):

 

 

Carrier Confirmation

One of the aspects of the FTL process is that carrier confirmation is a prerequisite for warehouse processing. The system will not release goods for picking and packing until the carrier has confirmed the Freight Order.

This design makes practical sense: in FTL, the entire truck is dedicated to your shipment. You want to be certain the truck is actually coming before you start staging goods at the dock. The confirmation flow works like this:

  1. Set the Freight Order to "Confirmation Pending" (send to carrier)
  2. Wait for the carrier to respond with "Confirmed" or "Rejected"
  3. Upon confirmation, the system automatically releases the associated Transportation Requests for warehouse processing

If the carrier rejects, the Freight Order is cancelled and a new freight order is created, where another carrier can be assigbed. This follows the pattern of the Direct LTL confirmation process.

Current Limitations

The FTL shipping process is actively evolving. Here are the current limitations you should be aware of:

  • Single pickup and single delivery only - Currently, each Freight Order supports one pickup stop and one delivery stop. Multi-stop scenarios (e.g., one truck picking up at multiple warehouses or delivering to multiple destinations) are not yet supported, though the data model is designed with this future capability in mind
  • No consolidation on Freight Order level - Each Transportation Request is assigned to its own Freight Order in a 1:1 relationship. There is no grouping engine that combines multiple TRs into a single Freight Order based on matching routes or time windows — even when it would make logistical sense to share a truck
  • No freight tendering integration - Unlike LTL consignments, Freight Orders cannot yet be sent through the freight tendering process to solicit competing quotes from multiple carriers. Carrier assignment is currently a direct, manual step
  • Manual subcontracting only - The subcontracting process for FTL is manual. While BN4L integration handles automated carrier communication for consignment-based processes, the Freight Order confirmation flow requires more manual interaction
  • No own-fleet support - The current FTL process is designed for subcontracted transportation only. Scenarios where you use your own vehicles to fulfill FTL shipments are not yet supported

 

References

  • Configure Shipping Processes (Processing Variants) - SAP Help Portal
  • FTL process configuration - SAP Help Portal
  • Direct LTL Shipping Process - SAP Help Portal
  • BN4L Integration Setup - SAP Help Portal
  • Processing Variants: The Idea Behind Configuration - SAP Community Blog
